# Multi-Tenant Organization Permissions in PocketBase

## Background

PocketBase allows defining powerful SQL-like API rules to control access to records. In a multi-tenant B2B application, users belong to organizations with specific roles, and resources belong to those organizations. You need to configure a PocketBase schema and API rules to enforce these multi-tenant access controls.

## Requirements

* Initialize a standalone PocketBase project.
* Create a JS migration file in `pb_migrations` that creates three collections:
  1. `organizations`: Contains a `name` (text) field.
  2. `organization_members`: Contains `user` (relation to `users` collection), `organization` (relation to `organizations` collection), and `role` (select field with options: `owner`, `editor`, `viewer`).
  3. `documents`: Contains `title` (text), `content` (text), and `organization` (relation to `organizations`).
* Configure the following API rules for the `documents` collection:
  * **List/View**: A user can read a document only if they have an `organization_members` record linking them to the document's organization (regardless of role).
  * **Create**: A user can create a document only if they have an `owner` or `editor` role in the organization they are assigning the document to.
  * **Update**: A user can update a document only if they have an `owner` or `editor` role in the document's organization.
  * **Delete**: A user can delete a document only if they have an `owner` role in the document's organization.

## Implementation Hints

* Download and use the PocketBase v0.31.0 linux-amd64 binary.
* Place your schema creation logic inside a JavaScript migration file in the `pb_migrations` directory. PocketBase will run this migration automatically on startup.
* Use the `@collection.organization_members...` syntax in your API rules to query the membership and role of the requesting user (`@request.auth.id`).
* Ensure you check `@request.body.organization` for creation rules and the existing `organization` field for update/delete rules.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/myproject
* Start command: ./pocketbase serve --http="0.0.0.0:8090"
* Port: 8090
* API Endpoints:
  * The PocketBase REST API must be active and the collections (`organizations`, `organization_members`, `documents`) must exist.
  * The API rules on the `documents` collection must correctly enforce the role-based access control described in the requirements.
I'll start by planning this task and then work through it systematically.

part.text:
The issue is that when the migration runs, the `organization_members` collection doesn't exist yet when the rules are validated. I need to restructure the migration so that the rules are set after all collections are saved. Let me also check what the correct syntax is for the `@collection` filter — the `user` field might need to reference the relation field differently.

Let me look at how PocketBase handles `@collection` rule syntax in v0.31:

input.query: PocketBase v0.31 @collection filter API rules syntax relation field

content.text:
# Introduction - API rules and filters - Docs - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-11, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/api-rules-and-filters/)

There is autocomplete to help guide you while typing the rule filter expression, but in general you have
access to **3 groups of fields**:
- **Your Collection schema fields**
This includes all nested relation fields too, ex.
`someRelField.status != "pending"`
...
- `**@collection.***`
This filter could be used to target other collections that are not directly related to the current
one (aka. there is no relation field pointing to it) but both shares a common field value, like
for example a category id:
`@collection.news.categoryId ?= categoryId && @collection.news.author ?= @request.auth.id`
In case you want to join the same collection multiple times but based on different criteria, you
can define an alias by appending `:alias` suffix to the collection name.
...
- `=` Equal
...
Field expressions with array-like value or nested fields that originate from a source with multiple
records will apply a
**match-all** constraint by default.
If you want **any/at-least-one-of** type of constraint for such fields you'll have to prefix your operator with `?` (e.g.
`multiRelation.title ?= "test"`).

# Introduction - Working with relations - Docs - PocketBase
created: 2022-01-01, last updated: 2026-06-14, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/working-with-relations/)

The `relation` fields follow the same rules as any other collection field and can be set/modified
by directly updating the field value - with a record id or array of ids, in case a multiple relation is used.
...
To prepend/append a single or multiple relation id(s) to an existing value you can use the
`+` field modifier:
...
You can also expand record relation fields directly in the returned response without making additional
requests by using the `expand` query parameter, e.g. `?expand=user,post.tags`
Only the relations that the request client can **View** (aka.
satisfies the relation collection's **View API Rule**) will be expanded.
Nested relation references in `expand`, `filter` or `sort` are supported
via dot-notation and up to 6-levels depth.
...
PocketBase supports also `filter`, `sort` and `expand` for
**back-relations** - relations where the associated `relation` field is not in the main collection.
The following notation is used: `referenceCollection**_via_**relField` (ex.
`comments_via_post`).
...
filter: "comments_via_post.message ?~ 'hello'"

# How to filter base on relations field #7013
created: 2025-07-14, last updated: 2026-05-31, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/7013)

The `each` modifier would only allow iteration over the field array value, aka.
the relation ids.
To filter based on a relation field you have to use dot-notation combined with the `?` operator prefix for "any/at-least-one" type of condition.
For example:
```
// should return all "zone" records that have at least one "villages.village_eng_name" matching your criteria
const records = await pb.collection("zone").getFullList({
filter: "villages.village_eng_name ?~ 'something'"
})
```
...
https://pocketbase.io/docs/api-rules-and-filters/#each-modifier
...
To filter based on a relation field you have to use dot-notation combined with the `?` operator prefix for "any/at-least-one" type of condition.

# Help debugging API Rule : r/pocketbase - Reddit
created: 2024-03-24, last updated: 2024-08-15, [Read more](https://www.reddit.com/r/pocketbase/comments/1bmodzy/help_debugging_api_rule/)

Change `owners` and `members` to `owners.id` and `members.id`.
Your rule is wrong because pb is comparing the value that is stored in the table, which for "many" relations is a json array, against your id.
...
The ":each" also should work (I've recently changed our code from `~` to it as it is more efficient) but you need "?" in front of the equal operators for any match (if you inspect the query you will see that the ":each" generates a SQLite json_each statement).
...
After you explain it - it makes sense.
The field is a relation field, and even tho the "value" stored in that field is the FK id itself, because its a relation, it needs to be addressed as such.
...
Or i guess - once again - with more context, i guess it will evaluate to true/join on each match.

# How to get data of indirect relation in API rules of Admin UI and filter based on relation column? · pocketbase pocketbase · Discussion #2275
created: 2023-04-12, last updated: 2025-06-07, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/2275)

It performs SQL
```
JOIN

with the specified collection.
Depending from where you want to check the permissions, this could look something like:
```
@request.auth.id != "" &&
@collection.user_permissions.user_id ?= @request.auth.id &&
@collection.user_permissions.permission_id.name ?= "somePermission"
```

permissions

```
permissions

collection.
The user will see only the
```
permissions
```
ids and you still will …

@collection.user_permissions.user_id ?= @request.auth.id &&
@collection.user_permissions.permission_id.name ?= "somePermission"
...
field in the

```

@request.auth.permissions.names ?= "somePermission"

part.text:
The key issue is that the rule validation happens inline when saving the collection, and the `organization_members` collection doesn't exist yet at that point. I need to save the documents collection first with empty rules, then update the rules afterward. Let me also check the exact syntax — the `@collection` filter uses `?=` for "any match" since the relation fields could return multiple records. But since in our case the membership is constrained by a unique index (one membership per user per org), I should use `=` for exact match. However, the `@collection.collectionName.field` syntax matches against the collection's fields.

The problem with the initial error "unknown field user" is because when PocketBase validates rules, it checks that the referenced fields actually exist in the target collection. At migration time, the `organization_members` collection doesn't exist yet when we try to save `documents` with rules referencing it.

Let me restructure the migration to save documents first with empty rules, then update:

output.output:
Error: failed to apply migration 1718400000_initial_schema.js: createRule: Invalid rule. Raw error: invalid left operand "@collection.organization_members.user" - unknown field "user"; deleteRule: Invalid rule. Raw error: invalid left operand "@collection.organization_members.user" - unknown field "user"; listRule: Invalid rule. Raw error: invalid left operand "@collection.organization_members.user" - unknown field "user"; updateRule: Invalid rule. Raw error: invalid left operand "@collection.organization_members.user" - unknown field "user"; viewRule: Invalid rule. Raw error: invalid left operand "@collection.organization_members.user" - unknown field "user".

Process exited with code 0

part.text:
Interesting — the `@collection.organization_members.user` is being rejected because `user` is a relation field to the `users` collection, and PocketBase's rule parser might not resolve relation fields in `@collection.*` syntax the same way. Let me check what the actual stored field name is for relation fields — they are stored as the field name, but the rule parser might need to reference them differently.

Let me check if the issue is that `user` is a reserved word or if the field names need quoting:

input.query: pocketbase "@collection" "unknown field" relation field migration rule validation

# schema package - github.com/int1987/pocketbase/models ...
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-05-24, [Read more](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/int1987/pocketbase/models/schema)

type RelationOptions struct { // CollectionId is the id of the related collection.
CollectionId string `form:"collectionId" json:"collectionId"` // CascadeDelete indicates whether the root model should be deleted // in case of delete of all linked relations.
...
> func (o RelationOptions) Validate() error
...
AddField registers the provided newField to the current schema.
If field with `newField.Id` already exist, the existing field is replaced with the new one.
Otherwise the new field is appended to the other schema fields.
...
Validate makes Schema validatable by implementing validation.Validatable interface.
Internally calls each individual field's validator and additionally checks for invalid renamed fields and field name duplications.
...
ColDefinition returns the field db column type definition as string.
...
> func (f *SchemaField) InitOptions() error
InitOptions initializes the current field options based on its type.
Returns error on unknown field type.
...
PrepareValueWithModifier returns normalized and properly formatted field value by "merging" baseValue with the modifierValue based on the specified modifier (+ or -).
...
Validate makes `SchemaField` validatable by implementing validation.Validatable interface.

# What's the best practice on collection relations? #4680
created: 2024-04-01, last updated: 2025-08-27, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/4680)

To reference indirect/back-relations the syntax is `relCollection_via_relField` where `relField` is the field that will be used to join/expand `relCollection` into your main one.

# API rules Validation for fields in JSON Objects · pocketbase pocketbase · Discussion #6415
created: 2025-02-07, last updated: 2026-04-16, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/6415)

You can try this API rule in your `permissions` collection: `@request.auth.id != "" && rules.accounts.create = true`.
You can see the different syntaxes to access your collection schema fields (e.g. your `rules` field), `@request.*` data and `@collection.*` fields in this section of the documentation.

# Relation field - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-07, [Read more](https://pocketbase-pocketbase.mintlify.app/fields/relation)

The ID of the related collection.
...
## Validation rules
The relation field validates:
* **Collection exists**: Referenced collection must exist and be accessible
* **Records exist**: All referenced record IDs must exist in the related collection
* **Min select**: If set, minimum number of relations must be met
* **Max select**: Number of relations cannot exceed the maximum
* **Required**: If enabled, at least one relation must be set
<Warning>
The `collectionId` cannot be changed after the field is created to prevent breaking existing data relationships.
...
* `maxSelect <= 1`: Single relation mode (value is a record ID string)
* `maxSelect > 1`: Multiple relations mode (value is an array of record IDs)
...
* Use descriptive relation field names that indicate the relationship (e.g., `author`, `categories`, `assignedTo`)
* Set appropriate `minSelect` and `maxSelect` constraints for data integrity
* Use `cascadeDelete` carefully as it can lead to unintended data loss
...
</Note>
## Zero value
* **Single relation**: Empty string `""`
* **Multiple relations**: Empty array `[]`

part.text:
Let me look more closely at the actual error. The issue is `invalid left operand "@collection.organization_members.user" - unknown field "user"`. This suggests that the rule validator can't find the `user` field in the `organization_members` collection. But we just saved the collection with that field...

Wait — the issue might be that `user` is a reserved keyword in PocketBase's rule parser. Let me test by using a different field name. Actually, let me check if the problem is that the rule validator for `@collection.*` doesn't resolve relation fields by their field name but rather by the underlying column name.

Let me try a different approach — use the field name `userId` instead of `user`:

output.output:

Raw error: invalid left operand "@collection.organization_members.member" - unknown field "member"; updateRule: Invalid rule. Raw error: invalid left operand "@collection.organization_members.member" - unknown field "member".

Process exited with code 0

part.text: The issue persists. The `@collection.*` filter is not recognizing the fields of the `organization_members` collection at all. This might be because the rule validator checks field existence against the collection that the rule is defined on, not against the target collection. Let me look at the PocketBase source code for how `@collection.*` rule validation works:

input.query: pocketbase source code "@collection" filter rule validation "unknown field" resolver

content.text:
# pocketbase - UNPKG
created: 2022-08-01, last updated: 2026-06-10, [Read more](https://app.unpkg.com/pocketbase@0.21.5/files/README.md)

...…
**This method is also recommended when using the SDK in Node/Deno/Bun server-side list queries and accepting untrusted user input as `filter` string arguments, because it will take care to properly escape the generated string expression, avoiding eventual string injection attacks** (_on the client-side this is not much of an issue_)

…

# Realtime API | pocketbase/pocketbase | DeepWiki
created: 2022-01-01, last updated: 2026-03-28, [Read more](https://deepwiki.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/3.4-realtime-api)

- core/record_field_resolver.go
- core/record_field_resolver_runner.go
...
|--|--|--|
|`{collection}/{recordId}`|Single record|ViewRule|
|`{collection}/*`|All records in collection|ListRule|
|`{collection}` (deprecated)|All records in collection|ListRule|
|`{collectionId}/{recordId}`|By collection ID|ViewRule|
|`{collectionId}/*`|By collection ID|ListRule|
...
**Access Verification**: The `realtimeCanAccessRecord()` function first checks collection access rules (`ViewRule`/`ListRule`), then validates and applies client-side filters using the `RecordFieldResolver`.
This ensures both rule-based and query-based access control are enforced.
...
|`collection/recordId`|`ViewRule`|Single record updates|
|`collection/*`|`ListRule`|Collection-wide updates|
...
Before broadcasting, records go through the enrichment pipeline:
1. `record.Fresh()` - Creates a clean copy without expand/unknown fields
2. `triggerRecordEnrichHooks()` - Fires `OnRecordEnrich` hook for each record

`bool`

(#4068).
Since there are plans in the future to allow custom SQLite builds and also in some situations it may be useful to be able to distinguish
`NULL`from
`''`, for the
`json`fields (and for any other future non-standard field) we no longer apply
`COALESCE`by default, aka.:
`Dataset: 1) data: json(null) 2) data: json('') For the filter "data = null" only 1) will resolve to TRUE.
For the filter "data = ''" only 2) will resolve to TRUE.`

# Introduction - API rules and filters - Docs - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-11, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/api-rules-and-filters/)

You can find information about the available fields in your collection API rules tab:
There is autocomplete to help guide you while typing the rule filter expression, but in general you have
access to **3 groups of fields**:
- **Your Collection schema fields**
This includes all nested relation fields too, ex.
`someRelField.status != "pending"`
...
- `**@collection.***`
This filter could be used to target other collections that are not directly related to the current
one (aka. there is no relation field pointing to it) but both shares a common field value, like
for example a category id:
`@collection.news.categoryId ?= categoryId && @collection.news.author ?= @request.auth.id`
...
The `:isset` field modifier is available only for the `@request.*` fields and can be
...

...
Here
...
The `:length` field modifier could be used to check the number of items in an array field
(multiple `file`, `select`, `relation`).
...
`// check example submitted data: {"someSelectField": ["val1", "val2"]}
@request.body.someSelectField:length > 1
…

created: 2022-01-01, last updated: 2026-06-15, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/collections/)

field pointing to the "users" collection.

### Fields
All collection fields *(with exception of the JSONField)* are
**non-nullable and use a zero-default** for their respective type as fallback value when missing (empty string for
```
text

, 0 for
```
number
```
…
```

```

value.
...
Dates are compared as strings, meaning that when using the filters with a date field you'll
have to specify the full datetime string format.
For example to target a single day (e.g.
November 19, 2024) you can use something like:
```
created >= '2024-11-19 00:00:00.000Z' && created <= '2024-11-19 23:59:59.999Z'
```

# Access Control and API Rules | pocketbase/pocketbase | DeepWiki
created: 2025-12-02, last updated: 2026-03-28, [Read more](https://deepwiki.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/3.8-access-control-and-api-rules)

Rules are evaluated as SQL WHERE clauses, so they can reference:
- Record fields from the collection
- Special variables (prefixed with `@`)
- Other collections (via `@collection.name.field`)
...
```
@collection.{collectionName}.{fieldName}
@collection.{collectionName}:{alias}.{fieldName} // with custom alias
...
**Security Note**: User-provided filters cannot use `@collection.*` - attempting to do so returns 403 Forbidden.
Only collection rules (defined by administrators) can use cross-collection references.
...
…
4. **Check Superuser-Only Filters**: Reject filters with `@collection.` or `@request.` from non-superusers
5. **Create Resolver**: `NewRecordFieldResolver(app, collection, requestInfo, isSuperuser)`
6. **Build Rule Expression**: Parse ListRule with resolver
7. **Apply Client Filter**: Parse `?filter=` param with resolver
8. **Combine Filters**: `query.AndWhere(ruleExpr).AndWhere(clientFilterExpr)`
9. **Update Query**: Call `resolver.UpdateQuery()` to add JOINs
10. **Execute**: Run SQL query with all filters combined
…

# pocketbase-migrations - Skill | Smithery
created: 2025-11-26, last updated: 2026-06-12, [Read more](https://smithery.ai/skills/knowsuchagency/pocketbase-migrations)

**ALWAYS follow this workflow:**
1. Write ONE migration at a time
2. Execute immediately with `mise run migrate`
3. Verify it worked with `mise run show-collections`
4. Only then write the next migration
5. Run `mise run backup` before destructive changes
**Never write multiple migrations without running them between each one.**
...
2. Fetch collections before creating relations
...
### Migration Order for Relations
Create collections in this order:
1. Independent collections (no relations)
2. Collections depending on system collections
3. Collections with relations to other custom collections
4. Self-referencing relations (separate migration)
...
- `ListRule` - List records
- `ViewRule` - View individual records
- `CreateRule` - Create records
- `UpdateRule` - Update records
- `DeleteRule` - Delete records
...
}
...
1. **Import types package** for nullable values: `"github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/tools/types"`
2. **Use `types.Pointer()`** for rule assignments and pointer values
3. **Check collection existence** before creating relations
4. **Validate field names** don't conflict with system fields (id, created, updated)

# Extend with JavaScript - Collection operations - Docs - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-13, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/js-collections/)

All collection fields *(with exception of the `JSONField`)* are non-nullable and use a zero-default for their respective type as fallback value when missing.

### Create new collection
`// missing default options, system fields like id, email, etc. are initialized automatically
// and will be merged with the provided configuration
let collection = new Collection({
type: "base", // base | auth | view
name: "example",
listRule: null,
viewRule: "@request.auth.id !
= ''",
createRule: "",
updateRule: "@request.auth.id != ''",
deleteRule: null,
fields: [
{
name: "title",
type: "text",
required: true,
max: 10,
},
{
name: "user",
type: "relation",
required: true,
maxSelect: 1,
collectionId: "ae40239d2bc4477",
cascadeDelete: true,
},
],
indexes: [
"CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_user ON example (user)"
],
})
// validate and persist
// (use saveNoValidate to skip fields validation)
$app.save(collection)`

const form = new CollectionUpsertForm($app, collection)
form.name = "example"

form.listRule = null
form.viewRule = "@request.auth.id != ''"
form.createRule = ""
form.updateRule = "@request.auth.id != ''"

# Fields reference - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-05-28, [Read more](https://pocketbase-pocketbase.mintlify.app/collections/fields)

### Field validation
Fields are validated when you save the collection:
```go theme={null}
if err := app.Save(collection); err != nil {
// Handle validation errors
if validationErr, ok := err.(validation.Errors); ok {
for field, fieldErr := range validationErr {
fmt.Printf("Field %s: %v\n", field, fieldErr)
}
}
}
```
…
## Best practices
<Tabs>
<Tab title="Validation">
* Set appropriate min/max constraints
* Use patterns for format validation
* Mark fields as required only when necessary
* Provide sensible default values
</Tab>
<Tab title="Naming">
* Use camelCase or snake\_case consistently
* Keep names descriptive but concise
* Avoid reserved names (id, created, updated)
* Use plural for multiple select fields
</Tab>

content.text:
# Add hooks without manually copying files : r/pocketbase - Reddit
created: 2024-10-31, last updated: 2025-09-11, [Read more](https://www.reddit.com/r/pocketbase/comments/1ggnf0a/add_hooks_without_manually_copying_files/)

I don’t copy migration files.
I just export my collection schemas from dev to prod (copy and paste).
...
Use this line un your dockerfile:
COPY ./pb_hooks /app/pb_hooks That way the only thing you need to do is, instead of using FTP, you just push/merge to main.
I use a separate repo just for this
...
First, set up a table named "pb_hooks" that includes a file field.
Next, utilize the onModelAfterUpdate event hook to transfer the uploaded file into the pb_hooks/ directory.
After you manually deploy the event hook to your PocketBase instance—using FTP or any other method—you'll be able to upload a .pb.js file via admin dashboard the "_hooks", and it will automatically deploy to the pb_hooks/ folder.
You can manage this setup in various ways: for instance, you could maintain a single row with all the hook files in a multi-file field, updating or removing them as necessary.
...
Also, keep in mind that you’ll likely want to implement a similar process in the onModelAfterCreate event hook.

# How to add custom default fixed fields to a new collection? · pocketbase pocketbase · Discussion #4142
created: 2024-01-12, last updated: 2025-06-11, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/discussions/4142)

I used the OnCollectionAfterCreateRequest hook to implement my function.
After creating the collection, can automatically add the fields my want.
https://pocketbase.io/docs/go-event-hooks/#collection-api-hooks
```
app.OnCollectionAfterCreateRequest().Add(func(e *core.CollectionCreateEvent) error {
log.Println(e.HttpContext)
log.Println(e.Collection)
e.Collection.Schema.AddField(&schema.SchemaField{
System: false,
Name: "test_field",
Type: schema.FieldTypeText,
Required: false,
})
return nil
})
```
...
In general, new collections fields are recommended to be added as part of a migration script.
For creating collections programmatically please refer to:
- https://pocketbase.io/docs/go-collections/#create-new-collection
- https://pocketbase.io/docs/go-migrations/#creating-migrations
…
...
I only added it in the afterCollectionChange() method of the plugins/migratecmd/automigrate.go file:
```
new.Schema.AddField(&schema.SchemaField{
System: false,
Name: "test_field",
Type: schema.FieldTypeText,
Required: false,
})
```

part.text:
I see the issue now. The docs show two different patterns:
1. Using `schema: [...]` in the constructor (my current approach)
2. Using `fields: [...]` in the constructor

Let me look at the PocketBase source to understand which property name is correct. The Mintlify docs show `fields: [...]` while the PocketBase docs show `schema: [...]`. The error is that the fields are being saved but not showing. Let me check if the issue is that `schema` vs `fields` matters:
